content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 21,
1994
John Torrance
City Attorney
City of Simi Valley
2929 Tapo Canyon Road
Siiui Valley,
CA
93063—2199
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-043a
Dear Mr. Torrance:
This is in response to your request for clarification of our
advice letter to you dated March 15,
1994
(No. A-94-043).
This
request is made on behalf of Siiui Valley Mayor Gregory Stratton
regarding his responsibilities under the conflict—of--interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
In your prior letter,
you asked whether Mayor Stratton could
participate in the city’s bond rating and insurance presentation
regarding the refinancing of the bonds.
We advised that since the
city’s bond rating and insurance presentation is integrally linked
to the previous decision to refinance the bonds and later
decisions concerning the approval of bond documents, the mayor is
prohibited from participating in or influencing the decisions by
participating in the presentation.
However,
as we discussed,
“making or participating in the
making of a governmental decision” does not include actions of
public officials which are solely ministerial.
While the Act does
not define “ministerial” actions,
we have said that where an
official has no discretion with respect to how the action would be
performed or what the results would be,
or acts pursuant to clear
objective criteria it would be a ministerial act.
(Kenny Advice
Letter,
No. A-93-265;
Smith Advice Letter,
No.
1-93-215; Miller
Advice Letter,
No.
1—93—098;
Cano Advice Letter,
No.
1—90-190.)
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-043a
Page
2
You have provided additional information showing that the
city council will be setting (without the mayor’s participation)
specific parameters with respect to the mayor’s presentation.
It
appears that the resolution in question will require that the
mayor’s conduct in the presentations be “carried out in a
prescribed manner not allowing for personal discretion.”
2
If this
is the case, the mayor’s involvement would be ministerial and
would not constitute participation in a governmental decision.
3
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59O1.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
2
Webster’s New World Dict.
(college ed.
1988)
p.
864.
Please note that the mayor would still be prohibited from using
his official position to influence the decision by contacting,
or
appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence,
any
member, officer,
employee or consultant of the city for the
purpose of influencing the decision.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of
1Oc per page.

sections.question: Whether Mayor Stratton's participation in the city's bond rating and insurance presentation regarding refinancing of bonds constitutes prohibited participation in a governmental decision, or whether such participation would be ministerial and therefore permissible.
sections.conclusion: If the city council resolution requires that the mayor's conduct in the presentations be "carried out in a prescribed manner not allowing for personal discretion," the mayor's involvement would be ministerial and would not constitute participation in a governmental decision. However, the mayor would still be prohibited from using his official position to influence the decision by contacting, appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the city.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Mayor Stratton participate in a bond rating and insurance presentation when the city council sets specific parameters for his conduct that leave no room for personal discretion, making it a ministerial act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mayor Stratton may participate in the bond presentation if the city council establishes specific parameters that eliminate personal discretion, making it ministerial conduct, provided he does not attempt to influence other decision-makers.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a clarification letter responding to a prior advice letter (A-94-043). Contains clear question and conclusion sections. Minor OCR artifacts noted (e.g., 'Siiui' instead of 'Simi' in header). The document provides substantive legal analysis with multiple cited precedents.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Mayor Stratton's participation in the city's bond rating and insurance presentation regarding refinancing of bonds constitutes prohibited participation in a governmental decision, or whether such participation would be ministerial and therefore permissible.

CONCLUSION: If the city council resolution requires that the mayor's conduct in the presentations be "carried out in a prescribed manner not allowing for personal discretion," the mayor's involvement would be ministerial and would not constitute participation in a governmental decision. However, the mayor would still be prohibited from using his official position to influence the decision by contacting, appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the city.

This request is made on behalf of Siiui Valley Mayor Gregory Stratton regarding his responsibilities under the conflict—of--interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 In your prior letter, you asked whether Mayor Stratton could participate in the city’s bond rating and insurance presentation regarding the refinancing of the bonds. We advised that since the city’s bond rating and insurance presentation is integrally linked to the previous decision to refinance the bonds and later decisions concerning the approval of bond documents, the mayor is prohibited from participating in or influencing the decisions by participating in the presentation. However, as we discussed, “making or participating in the making of a governmental decision” does not include actions of public officials which are solely ministerial. While the Act does not define “ministerial” actions, we have said that where an official has no discretion with respect to how the action would be performed or what the results would be, or acts pursuant to clear objective criteria it would be a ministerial act. (Kenny Advice Letter, No. A-93-265; Smith Advice Letter, No. 1-93-215; Miller Advice Letter, No. 1—93—098; Cano Advice Letter, No. 1—90-190.) 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-94-043a Page 2 You have provided additional information showing that the city council will be setting (without the mayor’s participation) specific parameters with respect to the mayor’s presentation. It appears that the resolution in question will require that the mayor’s conduct in the presentations be “carried out in a prescribed manner not allowing for personal discretion.” 2 If this is the case, the mayor’s involvement would be ministerial and would not constitute participation in a governmental decision. 3 If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322—59O1. Sincerely, Steven G. Churchwell General Counsel John W. Wallace Counsel, Legal Division 2 Webster’s New World Dict. (college ed. 1988) p. 864. Please note that the mayor would still be prohibited from using his official position to influence the decision by contacting, or appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the city for the purpose of influencing the decision. embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter clarifies whether Simi Valley Mayor Stratton can participate in a bond rating and insurance presentation. The FPPC concludes that if the city council sets specific parameters eliminating personal discretion, the participation would be ministerial and permissible under conflict-of-interest rules.
